content:
Let’s factor 391 first:
  391 = 17·23.

Write for any prime p 
   rp = max(vp(a),vp(b)) – max(vp(a),vp(391)).

Then 
  lcm(a,b)>lcm(a,391) ⇔ ∏p p^{rp}>1,
and negative rp can occur at p=17,23 unless “covered” by a or b.

The cheapest way to get a positive overall exponent‐sum is:

• Choose a to cover one of the two big primes, say a=23.  
  Then for p=23: max(vp(a),vp(b))=1, max(vp(a),vp(391))=1 ⇒ r23=0.  
  For p=17: since a has no 17, to avoid r17<0 we must include 17 in b ⇒ r17=1–1=0.  
  Now b still needs a “new” prime to make ∏p p^{rp}>1. The smallest choice is p=2.  

Hence the minimal b is 17·2=34.  Check:
  a=23, b=34
  lcm(23,34)=782, lcm(23,391)=391, 782>391.

No smaller b can work.  Answer:
\boxed{34}

Let’s factor 391 first:
  391 = 17·23.

Write for any prime p 
   rp = max(vp(a),vp(b)) – max(vp(a),vp(391)).

Then 
  lcm(a,b)>lcm(a,391) ⇔ ∏p p^{rp}>1,
and negative rp can occur at p=17,23 unless “covered” by a or b.

The cheapest way to get a positive overall exponent‐sum is:

• Choose a to cover one of the two big primes, say a=23.  
  Then for p=23: max(vp(a),vp(b))=1, max(vp(a),vp(391))=1 ⇒ r23=0.  
  For p=17: since a has no 17, to avoid r17<0 we must include 17 in b ⇒ r17=1–1=0.  
  Now b still needs a “new” prime to make ∏p p^{rp}>1. The smallest choice is p=2.  

Hence the minimal b is 17·2=34.  Check:
  a=23, b=34
  lcm(23,34)=782, lcm(23,391)=391, 782>391.

No smaller b can work.  Answer:
\boxed{34}
